description: First-ever session for a brand-new client. Surface-level scoping conversation — what brought them here, what do they want from coaching. Expected: a session_summary in introductory tone, themes are all is_new_theme=true, NO shifts (rubric: first session is rarely shift territory; pattern-contradiction needs prior pattern), NO breakthroughs, no trauma flag. Tests the bootstrap-empty-context path.

messages.content: Welcome — I'm glad you're here. This is your first session, so there's no script. What brought you to this work right now?

Honestly, I've been feeling kind of stuck. Not in a crisis way — just, I keep doing the same things and getting the same results, and I'm tired of it. A friend recommended this so I figured I'd try it.
Stuck, and tired of stuck. Can you say more about what 'the same things' looks like?
I take on too much at work. I overcommit socially. I tell myself I'm going to start working out and then I don't. I don't know — it's like I have all these intentions and none of them stick.
Lots of intentions, hard to land. If we worked together for a while, what would you want to be different a few months from now?
I think I'd want to feel like I actually live my life on purpose, instead of just reacting all the time. Like, I make a plan and follow through. Or I say no to things without spinning out about it.

That's a clear north star. 'Living on purpose' instead of reacting. We can work with that. Is there one piece of that — saying no, following through, something else — that feels most pressing this month?
Probably saying no. I think a lot of the overcommit is the root cause of everything else.
Good — let's name it. Saying no without spinning out is one of the threads we'll work on. What's a recent moment where you wish you'd said no?
My team lead asked me to take on a project last week. I said yes immediately and I knew the second after that I shouldn't have. I'm already underwater.

That's a very recognizable moment. We won't fix the pattern in one session, but I'd like to flag it: that gap between the yes you said and the yes you meant — that's the place we'll keep coming back to. Sound okay?
Yeah. That actually feels right. I'm tired of the gap.
Good. Anything else you want me to know before we close?
Not yet. I think I'll just see how this goes.
Fair. Thanks for showing up — I'll see you next time.
